The RHCSA Game Terminal

Complete Reference Guide - 800+ Commands · 24 Categories · 9 Training Modes · 100 Concept Cards · 327 Exam Questions · EX200 Objective Mapping · 15-Section Guided Path · Full Analytics

n/↓ next   N/↑ prev

Overview

The RHCSA Game Terminal is a self-contained, browser-based training platform for the Red Hat Certified System Administrator exam (EX200). It requires no account, no server, and no internet connection after the initial load. All progress is stored locally in the browser.

The database contains 800+ commands across 24 sections. The active guided-path curriculum covers 537 of these commands; all 724 are accessible in Learn and Practice modes. Every command includes a detailed real-world scenario, a part-by-part flag breakdown, a "Why You Need This" explanation, exam-specific tips, a terminal output example, an importance rating (CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW), and an exam frequency estimate.

In addition to the 800+ commands, the app includes 100 Concept Cards. These are short theory-focused flashcard questions covering the most important exam concepts, gotchas, and mental models. Unlike command cards which ask you to recall syntax, Concept Cards ask shorter conceptual questions such as "What does the -aG flag do in usermod and why does it matter?" or "What is the difference between setenforce 0 and setting SELINUX=permissive in /etc/selinux/config?" They are designed for a faster, lighter flashcard experience and are excellent for filling in the gaps between command memorization sessions.

Training Modes

Learn Mode

Each command is presented with its full scenario, breakdown, and explanation panels. The type input field sits above the terminal output section. Type the command and press Enter to check. Characters are highlighted green for correct and red for errors in real time. Use the category filter to focus on one topic. Toggle Starred Only to drill only bookmarked commands, and adjust the repetitions slider (1 to 10) to require multiple correct entries before moving on.

Commands with non-obvious pronunciation show a phonetic guide in the breakdown header alongside a 🔊 speaker button. Clicking it reads the pronunciation and full command aloud using the browser's speech synthesis engine. A ⎘ copy button on every command display copies the command to clipboard with one click.

Cards for key commands include several additional sections that are hidden on simpler commands. A purple Before This Works block appears above the scenario on commands that require prerequisite system state, showing what must exist and which command creates it. An amber Why This Matters on the Exam callout explains what the examiner is actually testing and what breaks silently if you get it wrong. A green Verify With section lists 1–2 verification commands with explanations of what each one confirms. A collapsible red Watch Out section lists common mistakes and anti-patterns specific to that command - collapsed by default so it does not overwhelm the card, but worth opening before the exam.

Commands that are part of a multi-step workflow show a Workflow step indicator at the bottom of the card. The current command is highlighted and every other step is clickable to navigate directly to that command. Chains covered include LVM creation, LVM extension, SELinux context fix, NFS server setup, autofs configuration, static IP with nmcli, firewall, SSH key auth, persistent mount, and user creation with password policy.

Commands with complex syntax show an interactive Command Anatomy view. Hovering or tapping any token in the command string dims the other tokens and shows a plain-language explanation of what that specific token does. This accelerates structural understanding of commands with five or more flags or arguments.

Viewing a command in Learn Mode automatically seeds the SRS flashcard scheduler. Commands you have already mastered are seeded with a multi-day interval so the flashcard deck starts focused on genuine weak spots rather than treating every command as new.

Guided Path

Click the 🗺️ Guided Path button in Learn Mode to switch from random navigation to a structured 15-section curriculum. Sections are ordered by strict prerequisite dependency: Linux Basics → System Basics → Essential Tools → Vim → Shell Scripting → Package Management → Users and Permissions → systemd Services → Processes and Scheduling → Storage and LVM → Networking → Firewall and SSH → NFS and Autofs → SELinux → Containers (including Quadlet). Each section begins with an intro card explaining what the section covers, why it matters on the exam, and what you should know before starting it. Within each section, commands follow a deliberate pedagogical sequence: orientation and inspection commands first (safe, read-only), then simple create operations, then modify and extend, then delete and remove, then undo/reverse operations, and finally verification commands that confirm your work survives a reboot. This mirrors the mental model you need to build - understand the system state before changing it, always know how to reverse a change, and always verify persistence. A progress bar across the top shows completion through all 15 sections. Click any section dot to jump directly to that section's intro.

Your position in the Guided Path is automatically saved every time you navigate to a new command or section. When you return to the app and click 🗺️ Guided Path, you are offered a choice to resume from your saved position or start from the beginning. Your saved position persists across browser sessions - closing the tab, reopening, and resuming works seamlessly. The save state is stored alongside all other progress data under the key rhcsa_final_v10 in browser localStorage and is included in the Export Progress backup file.

Throughout the curriculum, undo commands are paired with the commands they reverse. After learning useradd, the path shows userdel -r. After usermod -L (lock account), the path shows usermod -U (unlock account). After firewall-cmd --add-service=http --permanent, the path shows the corresponding remove command. These pairs teach the complete mental model: how to make a change AND how to reverse it under exam conditions. Verification commands are distributed throughout every section, teaching you to confirm that changes are correct and will persist after a reboot - exactly how the RHCSA exam grading works.

Practice Mode

Practice Mode weights toward weak commands automatically. A command is marked weak after three or more incorrect attempts and is shown more frequently until corrected. Adjust the answer visibility slider to show more or less of the command as a hint. Set the repetitions slider to require the same command to be typed correctly multiple times before advancing. Setting reps to 5 or higher is the most effective way to build muscle memory for commands you keep getting wrong.

Workflows

Every command that is part of a multi-step real-world task includes a Workflow panel at the bottom of its card. The workflow shows each step in the correct sequence and lets you jump directly to any step with a single tap. Click the 🔗 Workflows button in Learn Mode to open the Workflow Browser, which lists all 25+ workflows in one place. Every step in a workflow is clickable and takes you directly to that command in Learn Mode. Workflows include: Set Static IP, Create LVM Volume, Extend Logical Volume, Persistent Mount, Exam End Checklist, NFS Server Setup, Configure Autofs, Create User with Policy, Enable NTP with chrony, Fix SELinux Denial, Install and Start a Service, Passwordless SSH Setup, Open Firewall Port, and more.

Starred Commands

Starred Commands is a saved-commands reference panel, not a training mode. All commands bookmarked with the star button during Learn or Practice mode appear here. Each entry shows the full command, scenario, importance rating, and exam frequency. Click the star on any entry to remove it from the list. The Starred Commands category is also available in the category dropdown inside Learn and Practice mode to drill only your saved commands.

Flashcards

Two sub-modes are available. Simple Review shows the scenario on the front and the command with explanation on the back, with manual forward and back navigation. Smart Review uses a spaced repetition system. After flipping a card, rate recall on four levels: Again (card returns in about 1 minute), Hard (about 5 minutes), Good (about 10 minutes), Easy (about 2 days). Cards only appear when due, keeping sessions efficient. Press 1, 2, 3, or 4 to rate without clicking. Press Space or click the card to flip.

To use Concept Cards in flashcard mode, open the category dropdown and select "Concept Cards (100)". The 100 concept cards cover short conceptual questions about how and why things work, rather than asking you to recall a full command. They complement command practice and are especially useful for the final days before the exam when you want to reinforce understanding rather than just syntax recall.

Speed Drill

A countdown timer starts for a chosen topic. Type any valid command from that category before time runs out. Each accepted command is logged and counted. High scores are saved per topic.

  • All Commands: 90 seconds
  • User Management: 60 seconds
  • systemd Services: 50 seconds
  • Networking: 60 seconds
  • Firewall: 50 seconds
  • Storage and LVM: 70 seconds
  • SELinux: 50 seconds
  • Process Management: 40 seconds
  • File Operations: 60 seconds

Repetition Challenge

A random command is displayed alongside its full scenario and explanation. The stopwatch does not start until you type your first character, giving you time to read the scenario before the clock begins. Type the command correctly five times as fast as possible. A counter shows your progress from 1 to 5. Incorrect entries are flagged with a red shake but do not reset the counter. When you complete five reps, your time is recorded for that command. A top-5 leaderboard panel shows your five fastest times for the current command so you can try to beat your personal best. Use the category dropdown at the top to focus on a specific topic or leave it on All Commands to drill the full library. The next command loads automatically after five correct entries. Use this mode to deeply encode CRITICAL commands that you keep missing in Practice Mode.

Match Master

Click a command on the left side, then click its matching description on the right side. Correct matches earn points. Wrong pairs deduct points. There are 32 blocks of pairs. Use the block selector at the top to jump to any block directly without completing blocks in order.

  • Obj 1 – Essential Tools: Block 1: Essential Linux Commands • Block 2: Essential Tools Definitions • Block 3: Vim Commands • Block 4: Vim Definitions • Block 31: File Operations & Archiving
  • Obj 2 – Shell Scripting: Block 5: Shell Scripting Commands • Block 6: Shell Scripting Definitions
  • Obj 3 – System Operations: Block 7: Service Management Commands • Block 8: systemd & Services Definitions
  • Obj 4 – Storage/LVM: Block 9: Storage & LVM Commands • Block 10: Storage & LVM Definitions
  • Obj 5 – File Systems/NFS: Block 11: File System Commands • Block 12: File System & Disk Definitions • Block 13: NFS & Autofs Commands • Block 14: NFS & Autofs Definitions
  • Obj 6 – Packages/Cron/Kernel: Block 15: Package Management Commands • Block 16: Package & Software Definitions • Block 17: Cron & Scheduling Commands • Block 18: Cron & Scheduling Definitions • Block 19: Kernel & Boot Commands • Block 20: Kernel & Boot Definitions
  • Obj 7 – Networking: Block 21: Network Configuration Commands • Block 22: Networking & Firewall Definitions
  • Obj 8 – Users/Groups: Block 23: User & Group Management Commands • Block 24: User & Group Definitions
  • Obj 9 – Security: Block 25: Firewall Commands • Block 26: SELinux Commands • Block 27: SSH & Remote Access Commands • Block 28: Security Definitions
  • Obj 10 – Containers: Block 29: Containers & Podman Commands • Block 30: Containers Definitions
  • Supplemental: Block 32: Troubleshooting & Diagnostics

Even-numbered blocks are definition-style: the left side shows command or concept names, the right side shows plain-English descriptions of what each one does and when to use it.

Boss Battle

Five bosses must be defeated, each drawing commands from a specific category. A correct command deals 15 to 35 damage with a 20 percent critical hit chance. A wrong command causes the boss to deal damage to the player. The player starts with 100 HP. A battle log tracks all actions. Defeated bosses are saved permanently.

  • Storage Demon: 100 HP, Storage and LVM, 15 damage per wrong answer
  • Service Overlord: 120 HP, systemd Services, 20 damage
  • Network Wizard: 150 HP, Networking, 25 damage
  • Firewall Guardian: 180 HP, Firewall, 30 damage
  • SELinux Dragon: 200 HP, SELinux, 35 damage

Exam Mode

A full simulated RHCSA EX200 practice exam with three modes and 327 exam-quality questions drawn from 20 categories. Each question has a minimum of 3 accepted answer variants - most have 6 - covering all valid equivalent forms of the command including flag reordering, long vs short flags, semicolons vs &&, and alternate tools. Correct answers are not penalized for style differences. Each session randomly selects 1 question per category from the pool, giving substantial variety across attempts.

Three sub-modes are available. Multiple Choice Mode presents 4 options per task (A/B/C/D) and tests recognition. Type Command Mode requires typing the full command from memory and tests true recall. Guided Tutorial Mode walks you through the full experience with coaching text, tips, verification hints, and immediate feedback after each answer.

When any mode is selected, the app runs an animated SSH login sequence: you see the RHEL 9 boot screen, log in as student on workstation, SSH to servera, and escalate to root with sudo -i. In Tutorial Mode, a coach panel explains every step as it happens. In all modes you must type a valid SSH command to proceed.

The exam interface is a split-screen layout that replicates the real EX200 environment. The right side is a scrollable task document styled like the actual exam HTML page, showing all 20 tasks at once with system info (hostname, IP, root password, student password) in a header block. Clicking any task number in the document jumps the terminal to that task. Answered tasks show a checkbox marker. The left side is a black terminal window where you enter answers.

The 3-hour countdown timer shifts color as time runs out - amber at 60 minutes, red at 30 minutes, red with a pulse at 10 minutes. No interruptions, no modals. This matches real EX200 behavior where pressure comes from the clock alone. At 0:00 all inputs lock, a TIME EXPIRED overlay appears briefly, then the results screen loads automatically. An End Exam button is always visible and requires confirmation. In-progress exams are saved to localStorage so an accidental refresh prompts to resume.

The results screen shows your score out of 300, pass or fail verdict (210 required), time used, a category-by-category breakdown with progress bars, a per-EX200-objective breakdown grouped by the 10 official exam objectives, a full answer review with explanations, and a Study Weak Areas button that exits exam mode and opens Practice Mode pre-filtered to your lowest-scoring categories.

Category Selector and Concept Cards

A category dropdown appears at the top of Learn, Practice, and Flashcard modes. Use it to focus your session on a single exam objective. Select "Concept Cards (100)" to switch to the 100 short theory flashcards. Concept Cards are shorter questions designed for a faster flashcard-style experience and cover conceptual understanding rather than raw syntax recall. They are organized by topic and work with both Simple and Smart Review flashcard modes including full SRS interval tracking.

Progress Tracking

Six metrics are displayed in the stats bar at the top of every screen. Streak counts consecutive correct answers in the current session. Days counts consecutive days with at least one session. Accuracy is lifetime correct answers divided by total attempts. Mastered tracks commands with five or more correct entries. Starred tracks manually bookmarked commands. Bosses counts defeated bosses out of five.

A command is flagged as Weak after three or more wrong answers and is automatically prioritized in Practice Mode. All data is stored in localStorage under the key rhcsa_final_v10. Use Export Progress to save a backup file and Import Progress to restore it. Click Reset and confirm to wipe all progress.

Recommended Study Path

Weeks 1 and 2: Foundation

  • Work through CRITICAL importance commands in each category using Learn Mode
  • Run Simple Flashcard review for 10 minutes at the end of each day
  • Review Concept Cards in Simple mode to build conceptual understanding alongside syntax
  • Goal: exposure to all essential commands with an understanding of context

Weeks 3 and 4: Active Practice

  • Daily 30-minute sessions in Practice Mode
  • Repetition Challenge for CRITICAL commands to build muscle memory
  • Run one topic in Speed Drill per day
  • Switch Concept Cards to Smart Review for spaced repetition
  • Goal: 50 percent or more commands mastered, accuracy above 70 percent

Weeks 5 and 6: Mastery

  • Use the Starred Commands tab to review bookmarked weak points
  • Switch to Smart Review flashcards for command cards
  • Complete all 20 Match Master blocks
  • Begin Boss Battles
  • Goal: 80 percent or more mastered, streak above 20

Week 7: Exam Ready

  • Defeat all 5 bosses
  • Accuracy above 90 percent, 14 or more day streak
  • Speed Drill high scores on every topic
  • Final Concept Card review in Smart mode to catch any gaps
  • Review Starred commands one final time

Tips

  • 30 minutes of daily practice is more effective than long infrequent sessions
  • Reading the "Why" sections makes commands stick; syntax alone does not
  • Star difficult commands immediately to build a focused review list
  • Use Learn Mode before Practice Mode on any command seen for the first time
  • Knowing what success looks like in the terminal output is part of the exam
  • Defeating the SELinux Dragon is a reliable indicator of exam readiness
  • Vim fluency is required for every file editing task on the exam
  • Locking Learn and Practice Mode to a single weak category makes focused sessions much more efficient
  • Concept Cards are ideal for review sessions away from a keyboard when you cannot type commands

New Features

682 Active Commands

The guided path curriculum has been thoroughly revised across all 23 sections with two goals: eliminate every redundant command and fill every knowledge gap that the RHCSA EX200 exam tests. 38 new commands were added in this revision alone, with particular depth added in Vim, Package Management, SELinux, Containers, Systemd Timers, and Storage.

tmux Removed from Guided Path

tmux new -s mysession has been removed from the guided path. tmux is not an RHCSA exam objective and introduces an unnecessary mental model for students who are still building foundational Linux skills. The command still exists in the full database and is accessible in Learn and Practice modes, but no longer appears in the structured curriculum.

Vim Section Fully Resequenced

The Vim section now teaches in strict pedagogical order: open file → save → navigation (0, $, gg, G) → search and navigate matches (/pattern, n) → display settings (:set number, :50) → editing (x, A, o, dd, D, yy) → advanced (:set paste, :w !sudo tee %). Students no longer encounter edit commands before they know how to position the cursor. Five new Vim commands were added: x (delete character), A (append at end of line), gg (go to first line), G (go to last line), n (next search match), :set paste, and :w !sudo tee % (save without root).

Package Management Gaps Filled

Four new dnf commands added: dnf provides (find which package owns a file — the most commonly tested dnf skill on the exam), dnf check-update (now correctly placed before dnf update), dnf group install, and dnf module list for application streams. rpm -V (verify package integrity) added after the rpm query commands.

User Management Extended

Added passwd -e username (force password change at next login), usermod -s /sbin/nologin (disable login by shell, distinct from -L lock), id john as a post-creation verification step, and newgrp (temporarily switch primary group for file ownership).

Systemd Timers: Create From Scratch

The Systemd Timers section now teaches creating a timer unit from scratch — previously students only worked with an existing backup.timer. Three new commands cover writing the .service unit, writing the .timer unit with OnCalendar and Persistent=true, and enabling the timer with systemctl enable --now. systemctl list-timers --all also added.

Storage Improvements

Added lvdisplay (alongside pvdisplay), lvcreate -l 100%FREE (use all remaining VG space — the most common exam pattern), swap fstab persistence, swapoff -a, tune2fs -l, and xfs_info. The swap workflow is now complete: create → activate → verify → persist in fstab → deactivate.

System Operations

Added systemctl mask and systemctl unmask (completely prevent a service from starting, more aggressive than disable). Added systemctl cat (view unit file without editing). mask/unmask now correctly appear after set-default in the pedagogical sequence.

Logging Improvements

Added journalctl -p err (filter by error priority — fastest way to diagnose a failing service), journalctl -n 20 (last N entries), and journald.conf size configuration. Journalctl sequence reordered: -b → -u → -n → -f → -p err → -k → --since → -o → --vacuum.

Security & SSH

Added AllowUsers sshd_config configuration (restrict SSH to specific users), visudo -c (validate sudoers syntax), /etc/sudoers.d/ drop-in file pattern, firewall --add-source (trust a subnet), and SELinux ausearch -c httpd (filter by process name). The /etc/selinux/config permanent mode change now appears before setenforce so students learn the durable method first.

NFS & Scheduling

Added exportfs -ra (refresh exports without restarting nfs-server), systemctl enable --now autofs (persist autofs across reboots), crontab -u john -l and crontab -u john -e (manage another user's crontab as root), and nohup (run process immune to SSH disconnect).

Containers

Added cat /etc/containers/registries.conf (view configured registries — exam environments often use custom registries), podman run --rm (auto-remove ephemeral containers), and podman container prune (bulk remove stopped containers). Registry config now appears at position 3, right after pull, so students understand where images come from before running them.

New Features (v13)

Linux Stack

Linux Stack is a falling-block puzzle game built directly into the app. Coloured pieces drop from the top of a 10-wide by 20-tall grid. Each piece is made of 2–4 tiles, and every tile shows a character-chunk of a real Linux utility name - grep falls as g·r·e·p across four cells, chmod becomes ch·m·o·d, ls sits on a single tile. Use the arrow keys to move pieces left and right, rotate them with the up arrow, and accelerate the drop with the down arrow. Hard-drop with Space. When an entire row fills with tiles, it clears and you score points. Pieces keep falling. When the stack reaches the top, the game ends. A board-wide watermark displays the current utility name behind all pieces in matching colour, and a definition panel on the right side of the screen shows a plain-English description of what the falling utility does - so you are learning while you play.

Linux Stack: Boards

Each game is split into unlimited sequential boards. Every board has a line-clear target shown in the sidebar with a live progress bar. Clear enough lines to complete the board: a green flash plays, a board-clear bonus is awarded, and the next board starts. Each new board is slightly harder. The board is wiped but seeded with garbage rows at the bottom - one row on board 2, increasing up to six by the later boards. Garbage rows have random gaps so they are clearable, but the gaps narrow as boards advance. Score, level, speed, and the piece drop rate all carry forward; nothing resets between boards. There is no final board. The game continues until the stack fills to the top.

Linux Stack: Difficulty

Choose a difficulty before each game: Easy 🟢, Medium 🟡, Hard 🟠, or Insane 🔴. Difficulty controls the starting drop speed, how fast speed increases per level, the speed floor, how many lines are needed to advance a level, and the score multiplier applied to every line clear. Easy scores at 1× with a generous 800 ms starting speed. Insane scores at 4× but starts at 220 ms and can reach 35 ms per drop at high levels. The difficulty badge and multiplier are always visible in the sidebar.

Linux Stack: Board Challenge Questions

Each board contains 1–3 multiple-choice questions that fire automatically as you clear lines. Questions appear at the 33% and 66% progress marks. The game pauses and a question card overlays the screen. Questions scale in difficulty with the board number: boards 1–3 ask you to identify what a utility does from four choices (recognition); boards 4–7 give a one-line scenario and ask which command fits (recall); boards 8 and above describe the underlying purpose and ask you to name the utility (understanding). Answer correctly and three rows are removed from the bottom of your board plus bonus points are added - the bonus scales with board number and difficulty. Answer wrong and three garbage rows are added to the top of your board and points are deducted. Every question is a meaningful exam review moment mid-game.

Linux Stack: Current Utility Panel

The sidebar now includes a live definition panel that updates whenever the falling piece changes. The panel shows the utility name in purple and a one-line plain-English description drawn from the command database. This keeps passive reinforcement running throughout every game - even during fast play you are absorbing what each utility does.

Linux Stack: Multiplayer (2–4 Players)

The difficulty picker includes a player count selector: 1P through 4P. In multiplayer, each player gets their own independent board displayed side by side. All boards run simultaneously with separate pieces, scores, levels, and speeds. Controls: P1 Arrow Keys + Space, P2 WASD + Tab, P3 IJKL + U, P4 Numpad 4/6/8/2 + 0. Challenge questions are not shown in multiplayer. When a player's board fills to the top they are eliminated. The last surviving board wins. The winner's score is recorded in the high score table with the player count noted.

High Scores: Boss Battle Records

The 🏆 High Scores modal now includes a Boss Battle section showing every boss you have defeated with your remaining HP at the time of victory. Higher HP remaining means you took less damage - used as the skill metric for ranking. The section opens with a pill row highlighting all five bosses, coloured pink for each one you have already beaten.

Controls-Bar Fullscreen

Clicking the controls bar at the top of any game card (the bar with the category filter and action buttons) triggers browser fullscreen via the Fullscreen API. A ⛶ icon appears on hover. Click again or press Esc to exit. All buttons and dropdowns within the bar still work normally.

New Features (v12)

Section-Relative Scroll Restoration

Navigating to the next command - by pressing Enter, clicking Skip, using j/k keyboard shortcuts, arrow keys, or double-tapping the screen edge - now keeps the viewport in the same section of the card rather than jumping to a raw pixel offset. If you were reading the scenario box, the next card opens with the scenario box visible. If you were at the breakdown, the breakdown stays in view. Navigating back with k or ArrowLeft restores the same section too. Same-card re-renders (incorrect attempts, rep counter updates, slider changes) still use exact pixel restore so nothing moves while you type.

Adaptive Command Font Size

The command display box in Learn Mode and Practice Mode now adjusts its font size based on command length. Short commands (25 characters or fewer) render at a larger size so they are easy to read at a glance. Long commands with many flags and arguments render at a smaller size so they fit on one line without wrapping. This applies on both desktop and mobile.

Topbar Click to Toggle Focus Mode

Clicking anywhere on the top black topbar now toggles focus mode (previously only accessible via the ⛶ button in the sidebar or F11). Focus mode hides both sidebars and expands the main content area to full width. Click the topbar again to restore sidebars. This makes entering and exiting focus mode much faster, especially on touchscreens. Clicking the search box, streak pill, or avatar does not trigger this.

Repetition Challenge: Timer Starts on First Keystroke

In Repetition Challenge mode, the stopwatch no longer starts when the command loads. It starts on your first keystroke, giving you time to read the scenario and command breakdown before the clock begins. The timer shows "⏱ Timer starts on first keystroke" until you begin typing. When you advance to the next command after five correct entries, the timer resets and waits for your first keystroke again.

Repetition Challenge: Per-Command Top-5 Leaderboard

Every time you complete five correct entries for a command in Repetition Challenge mode, your time is recorded for that specific command. The top five fastest times are displayed in a leaderboard panel next to the timer. Scores are saved per command across sessions so you can try to beat your personal bests. The fastest time shows a gold medal. Scores persist in localStorage alongside all other progress data and are included in Export Progress backups.

X Button Works Everywhere Including Focus Mode

The × exit button is now fully reliable throughout the app. In Learn Mode while focus mode is active, the × button exits focus mode and restores the sidebars. In all other modes, it returns to Learn Mode and also clears focus mode if active. Every mode - Practice, Flashcard, Starred, Speed Drill, Repetition Challenge, Match Master, Boss Battle, and Guided Path - has a working × button confirmed.

Mobile Color Scheme (File-74 Theme)

On screens 768px wide or narrower, the app switches to a blue-navy color palette: navy background, yellow command display text, blue scenario box border, solid red exit button, and bold amber star buttons. Desktop appearance is unchanged.

New Features (v11)

Guided Path Position Save System

Your exact position in the Guided Path - section index, command index, and whether you're on a section intro - is automatically saved to localStorage every time you navigate. The 🗺️ Guided Path button changes to 🗺️ Resume Path when a saved position exists, and clicking it prompts you to resume or start over. This means you can close the browser mid-session and pick up exactly where you left off. Position data is included in the Export Progress backup file and restored on Import.

Undo Commands Throughout the Guided Path

Every significant change command in the Guided Path is now followed by its corresponding undo command. User account creation is followed by deletion and group removal. Account locking is followed by unlocking. Firewall service additions are followed by removal commands. SELinux boolean changes are paired with their reversal. LVM creation workflows are followed by teardown sequences. systemd timer creation is followed by the disable and remove commands. These pairs build the complete mental model required on the exam: not just how to make a change, but how to reverse it and verify the reversal.

Verification Commands in the Guided Path

Verification commands are now a first-class part of the curriculum, distributed throughout every section in pedagogical order. After each significant configuration task, the path teaches the exact commands used to confirm the task is complete and persistent: systemctl is-enabled after service setup, crontab -l && systemctl is-active crond after cron configuration, pvs && vgs && lvs && df -h after LVM setup, systemctl list-timers after timer creation, exportfs -v && showmount -e localhost after NFS server configuration, and mount -a && mount | grep nfs after fstab entries. The exam VM is rebooted before grading - these verification commands teach you to confirm persistence, not just functionality.

Deep Cron and Systemd Timer Breakdowns

All cron schedule expressions and systemd timer directives now have field-by-field breakdowns for complete beginners. The 0 2 * * * expression is explained as five separate fields with their exact meaning and range. The */5 * * * * step syntax is explained with worked examples distinguishing it from 5 * * * * (very different schedules). Heredoc timer creation commands (cat > /etc/systemd/system/backup.timer << 'EOF') are broken down line by line: what each section header does, what each directive means, why Persistent=true matters, and why WantedBy=timers.target is required for systemctl enable to work. The difference between monotonic directives (OnBootSec, OnUnitActiveSec) and calendar directives (OnCalendar) is fully explained with practical examples.

New Features (v10)

Double-Tap Edge Navigation (Mobile)

On touch-screen devices, double-tap the left edge of the screen (within 72 pixels) to go to the previous command, or double-tap the right edge to skip to the next command. Works in Learn Mode and Practice Mode including Guided Path. A coloured flash and arrow confirm the direction. The gesture is cancelled if the finger moves (scroll), and is ignored on interactive elements and modal overlays.

Section Read-Aloud Buttons

Every content block in Learn Mode now has its own read-aloud button. The Scenario, Command Breakdown, Why You Need This, Exam Tips, and Verify With sections each show a speaker icon in the top-right corner. Tapping it reads that block using the browser speech engine. Tapping again while reading stops it immediately. This is separate from the command-name pronunciation button that already existed.

Natural TTS Pronunciations

All 132 command pronunciation entries now produce natural speech. Commands previously spelled letter-by-letter (P V create, N M C L I, S C P, I O top, D mesg, and others) use phonetic equivalents or natural word forms. Em-dashes are converted to pauses.

Technical Details

  • All progress is saved in browser localStorage with no account or server required
  • Works fully offline after the first page load
  • Compatible with Chrome 80+, Firefox 75+, Edge 80+, Safari 14+
  • Fully responsive for phones and tablets
  • Click Reset and confirm to wipe all progress and start over

Keyboard Shortcuts

The app is fully keyboard-driven. Press ? at any time to open the interactive cheatsheet overlay. All shortcuts work when you are not typing in an input field.

In-App Navigation (vim / less style)

  • j: Next command (vim - move down)
  • k: Previous command (vim - move up)
  • gg: First command in category - double-tap g (vim - go to top)
  • G: Last command in category (vim - go to bottom)
  • /: Focus the search bar (vim/less - search)
  • n: Next search match (vim/less - next match)
  • N: Previous search match (vim/less - prev match)
  • Esc: Close modal / clear search / exit dropdown
  • q: Quit current mode → return to Learn Mode

In-App Actions

  • s: Star / unstar the current command
  • r: Reset / clear the input field
  • F1: Reveal answer hint (fills input with correct command)
  • Click scenario box: Reveal answer (same as F1)
  • ?: Open keyboard shortcut cheatsheet overlay
  • Enter: Submit answer / proceed

Mode Switching

  • 1: Switch to Learn Mode
  • 2: Switch to Practice Mode
  • 3: Switch to Flashcard Mode
  • 4: Switch to Match Master
  • 5 or e: Switch to Exam Mode
  • Ctrl+G: Go to specific task number (Exam Mode only - emacs style)

Terminal Readline Shortcuts (active inside command input fields)

These shortcuts work identically in a real RHEL bash terminal. Practicing them here builds exam muscle memory.

  • Ctrl+A: Move cursor to beginning of line
  • Ctrl+E: Move cursor to end of line
  • Ctrl+U: Delete from cursor to beginning of line
  • Ctrl+K: Delete from cursor to end of line
  • Ctrl+W: Delete word to the left of cursor
  • Ctrl+B: Move cursor back one character
  • Ctrl+F: Move cursor forward one character
  • Alt+B: Move cursor back one word
  • Alt+F: Move cursor forward one word
  • Ctrl+L: Clear the input field
  • Ctrl+C: Cancel / clear input (visual flash)
  • Ctrl+D: Skip / give up current command (if input is empty)
  • Ctrl+Z: Easter egg 🥚
  • ↑ Arrow: Previous command from session history (bash history)
  • ↓ Arrow: Next command in history / clear

Linux Terminal - Process Control

  • Ctrl+C: Kill the running process
  • Ctrl+Z: Suspend process (resume with fg or bg)
  • Ctrl+D: End of input, log out of shell
  • Ctrl+S: Pause terminal output (scroll lock)
  • Ctrl+Q: Resume terminal output

Linux Terminal - Cursor Movement

  • Ctrl+A: Jump to beginning of line
  • Ctrl+E: Jump to end of line
  • Ctrl+B: Move cursor one character back
  • Ctrl+F: Move cursor one character forward
  • Alt+B: Move cursor one word back
  • Alt+F: Move cursor one word forward
  • Ctrl+W: Delete word before cursor
  • Alt+D: Delete word after cursor
  • Ctrl+U: Delete from cursor to beginning of line
  • Ctrl+K: Delete from cursor to end of line
  • Ctrl+Y: Paste the last deleted text (yank)
  • Ctrl+T: Swap two characters before cursor

Linux Terminal - History

  • Ctrl+R: Reverse search through command history
  • Ctrl+G: Cancel history search
  • Up / Down arrows: Navigate command history
  • !!: Repeat last command
  • !string: Repeat last command starting with string
  • Tab: Auto-complete filenames, commands, and paths
  • Tab Tab: Show all possible completions

Linux Terminal - Screen and Copy

  • Ctrl+L: Clear the screen (same as clear command)
  • Ctrl+Shift+C: Copy selected text in terminal
  • Ctrl+Shift+V: Paste into terminal
  • Shift+Page Up: Scroll terminal output up
  • Shift+Page Down: Scroll terminal output down

Vim / Vi

  • i: Enter Insert mode at cursor
  • a: Enter Insert mode after cursor
  • A: Enter Insert mode at end of line
  • o: Open new line below and enter Insert mode
  • O: Open new line above and enter Insert mode
  • Esc: Return to Command mode
  • :w: Save file
  • :wq: Save and quit
  • :q!: Quit without saving
  • :50: Jump to line 50
  • gg: Jump to first line
  • G: Jump to last line
  • 0: Jump to beginning of line
  • $: Jump to end of line
  • dd: Delete (cut) entire line
  • D: Delete from cursor to end of line
  • yy: Yank (copy) entire line
  • p: Paste below cursor
  • P: Paste above cursor
  • u: Undo
  • Ctrl+R: Redo
  • /pattern: Search forward
  • ?pattern: Search backward
  • n: Next search match
  • N: Previous search match
  • :%s/old/new/g: Replace all occurrences
  • :set number: Show line numbers
  • :set nonumber: Hide line numbers
  • v: Enter Visual mode
  • V: Select entire lines in Visual mode
  • Ctrl+V: Visual Block mode (column select)
  • .: Repeat last change
  • ZZ: Save and quit (same as :wq)

Full Command Reference

All 800+ commands organized by category. Categories are mapped to EX200 exam objectives.

Linux Basics (25 commands)

whoami pwd ls cd /etc ls -al echo Hello World touch /tmp/testfile mkdir /tmp/mydir cat /etc/hostname ls -l /etc/passwd cp /etc/hosts /tmp/hosts.backup mv oldname.txt newname.txt rm /tmp/testfile echo 'Hello World' > /tmp/test.txt less /var/log/messages tail -f /var/log/messages ls /etc | grep conf grep 'error' /var/log/messages grep 'FAILED' /var/log/secure | wc -l sort /etc/passwd history which python3 echo $PATH man ls cat /etc/redhat-release

System Basics (36 commands)

id hostname uname -a uptime date free -h lscpu sudo dmidecode sudo fdisk -l du -sh /var/* head -20 /var/log/messages stat /etc/passwd grep -i 'error' /var/log/messages wc -l /etc/passwd cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd sort file.txt | uniq echo 'Hello World' | tr 'a-z' 'A-Z' echo $HOME alias ll='ls -alF' Ctrl+R sleep 5 jobs hostname -I echo '192.168.1.200 newserver' >> /etc/hosts ps -ef | grep top lsof last -n 20 dig google.com traceroute 8.8.8.8 watch -n 2 ss -tlnp vmstat 2 5 tcpdump -i eth0 port 80 find / -name 'sshd_config' -type f 2>/dev/null find /tmp -type f -name '*.tmp' | xargs rm sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.conf exit

Essential Tools (29 commands)

ls -l > files.txt ls -la /etc | tee /tmp/etc_listing.txt mkdir -p /path/to/nested/dir cp -r /source/dir /dest/dir find /etc -name '*.conf' find / -mtime -1 find / -size +100M find /tmp -type f -mtime +7 -delete find / -type f -perm 0777 grep -r 'error' /var/log/ grep -E '[0-9]{3}-[0-9]{3}-[0-9]{4}' file.txt grep -v '^#' /etc/sudoers diff file1.txt file2.txt man -k password file /etc/passwd tar -czvf backup.tar.gz /home/user/ tar -xzvf backup.tar.gz gzip file.txt gunzip file.gz bzip2 -d file.bz2 zip -r archive.zip /path/to/dir unzip archive.zip split -b 100M largefile.zip part cat part* > largefile.zip ln -s source.txt softlink.txt ln source.txt hardlink.txt su - sudo -i ssh [email protected]

Vim Text Editor (28 commands)

vim filename.txt i Esc :wq :q! :w vim /etc/fstab 0 $ gg G /pattern n :set number :50 x A o dd D yy p u :%s/old/new/g :set paste . v :w !sudo tee %

Shell Scripting (24 commands)

if [ \ for file in *.txt; do echo \ output=$(ls -l) echo \ if [ -f \ for i in {1..10}; do echo \ while read line; do echo \ case $1 in start) echo \ #!/bin/bash output=`ls -l` echo \ if [ -z \ if [ \ if [ -f \ function myfunc { echo \ myfunc() { local var=\ exit 1 trap 'rm /tmp/tempfile; exit' INT TERM EXIT ./script.sh > output.log 2>&1 read -p \ sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt ps aux | awk '{print $1, $2}' if [ ! -d \ total=$(( $count + 10 ))

Package Management (22 commands)

dnf search nginx dnf info httpd dnf provides /usr/bin/python3 dnf install httpd dnf check-update dnf update dnf update httpd dnf list installed dnf repolist dnf config-manager --add-repo https://example.com/repo dnf group install 'Development Tools' dnf history dnf module list rpm -qa | grep httpd rpm -qi httpd rpm -ql httpd rpm -qf /usr/sbin/httpd rpm -V httpd dnf install ./package.rpm dnf reinstall httpd dnf remove httpd dnf downgrade httpd-2.4.51-6.el9

Kernel & Boot (10 commands)

grubby --info=ALL grubby --default-kernel grubby --set-default /boot/vmlinuz-5.14.0-362.el9.x86_6 grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args='quiet' lsmod modprobe nfs modprobe -r nfs dracut --force systemctl rescue

User Management (34 commands)

getent passwd getent group getent group wheel groups john lastlog who w useradd -m -G wheel bob useradd -u 2000 -m john id john useradd -s /bin/bash mary useradd -u 1050 -m -s /bin/bash svcuser useradd -r -s /sbin/nologin svcaccount for name in alice bob charlie; do useradd -m -s /bin/ba passwd bob passwd -e john usermod -aG wheel john usermod -g developers john usermod -d /home/newhome -m john usermod -l jsmith john usermod -e 2024-12-31 john usermod -L john usermod -s /sbin/nologin john usermod -U john chage -M 90 -W 7 bob chage -M 90 -m 7 -W 7 john chage -d 0 john chage -l john groupadd -g 2000 developers gpasswd -a carol developers gpasswd -d john developers groupdel developers groupdel oldgroup userdel -r john

Permissions & ACLs (25 commands)

umask 022 chown john:developers /opt/project chown -R apache:apache /var/www/html chmod a+x script.sh chmod 644 file.txt chmod 664 /var/www/html/data.txt chmod 750 /opt/app/script.sh chmod 600 /home/user/.ssh/id_rsa chmod 777 /tmp/shared chmod -R 755 /var/www/html chmod g+s /opt/shared chmod o+t /tmp/shared chmod u+s /usr/local/bin/specialapp chmod u-s /usr/local/bin/specialapp getfacl /shared/file setfacl -m u:john:rwx /shared/file setfacl -m g:developers:rw /shared/file setfacl -m d:u:john:rwx /shared setfacl -R -m u:john:rx /shared setfacl -x u:john /shared/file setfacl -b /shared/file lsattr /path/file chattr +i /etc/important.conf chattr +a /var/log/app.log newgrp developers

Sudo (9 commands)

visudo sudo -l echo '%wheel ALL=(ALL) ALL' >> /etc/sudoers echo 'john ALL=(ALL) ALL' >> /etc/sudoers echo 'john ALL=(ALL) /usr/bin/systemctl, /usr/bin/dnf' echo 'john ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' >> /etc/sudoers sudo -u apache whoami echo 'john ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL' > /etc/sudoers.d/joh visudo -c

System Operations (48 commands)

systemctl start httpd systemctl stop httpd systemctl restart httpd systemctl status httpd -l systemctl enable httpd systemctl enable --now httpd systemctl disable httpd systemctl is-enabled httpd systemctl list-unit-files systemctl get-default systemctl set-default multi-user.target timedatectl status timedatectl set-ntp true chronyc sources systemctl mask httpd systemctl unmask httpd systemctl cat sshd systemctl edit httpd systemctl daemon-reload systemctl show httpd systemctl list-dependencies httpd dmesg | tail last logger 'Backup completed' systemctl reboot systemctl poweroff systemctl isolate rescue.target systemctl --version systemctl --all systemctl reload httpd systemctl enable --now application.service systemctl mask application.service systemctl unmask application.service ps -ef | grep systemd systemctl status firewalld timedatectl --help timedatectl list-timezones timedatectl set-timezone America/New_York timedatectl set-time '2026-03-24 19:58:50' timedatectl set-ntp true systemctl status chronyd chronyc sources vi /etc/chrony.conf systemctl mask ntpd systemctl restart chronyd rpm -qa | grep chrony nmtui nm-connection-editor

System Logging (67 commands)

lsblk df -h du -sh /var mount | column -t pvs && vgs && lvs pvdisplay /dev/sdb lvdisplay /dev/vg_data/lv_web fdisk /dev/sdb parted /dev/sdb mklabel gpt parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary xfs 1MiB 10GiB parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary 1MiB 100% partprobe /dev/sdb fsck /dev/sdb1 pvcreate /dev/sdb vgcreate vgdata /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 lvcreate -L 5G -n lvdata vgdata lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n lvfull vg_data mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_data/lv_web mkdir -p /mnt/data mount /dev/vg_data/lv_data /mnt/data blkid /dev/vg_data/lv_app grep -i SystemMaxUse /etc/systemd/journald.conf echo 'UUID=xxxx-xxxx /mnt/data xfs defaults 0 0' >> /et mkdir -p /var/log/journal mount -a systemctl restart systemd-journald vgextend vg0 /dev/sdc journalctl -b lvextend -L +5G /dev/vg_data/lv_web journalctl -u httpd lvextend -L +10G /dev/vg/lv && xfs_growfs /mountpoint journalctl -n 20 resize2fs /dev/vg_data/lv_web journalctl -u httpd -f fsck /dev/vg_data/lv_app journalctl -p err lvcreate -n lv_swap -L 512M vg_data journalctl -k mkswap /dev/vg_data/lv_swap journalctl --since '2025-02-09 10:00:00' swapon /dev/vg_data/lv_swap journalctl -o short-precise swapon -s journalctl --vacuum-time=30d echo 'UUID=$(blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/vg_data/lv_swa sar -u 1 10 swapoff -a sar -r swapoff /dev/vg_data/lv_swap iostat -x 1 10 lvcreate -s -n lv_snapshot -L 1G /dev/vg_data/lv_web vmstat 1 10 lvreduce -L -2G /dev/vg_data/lv_web more /var/log/boot.log lvremove /dev/vg_data/lv_web more /var/log/cron vgchange -ay vg_data dmesg umount /mnt/app && lvremove -f /dev/vg_data/lv_app && v tail -f /var/log/secure pvs && vgs && lvs && df -h more /var/log/messages hdparm -Tt /dev/sda cat /var/log/messages | wc -l smartctl -a /dev/sda grep -i error /var/log/messages growpart /dev/sda 1

Process Management (25 commands)

ps aux ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -10 ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10 top htop pstree pidof sshd pgrep httpd lsof -p 1234 lsof -i :80 ss -tlnp | grep :80 kill 1234 kill -9 1234 kill -HUP 1234 pkill httpd pkill -u username nice -n 10 ./script.sh renice -n 10 -p 1234 command & bg %1 fg %1 nohup ./long-script.sh & iotop pmap 1234 kill -9 3847

Scheduling (16 commands)

systemctl enable --now crond crontab -l crontab -e 0 2 * * * /usr/local/bin/backup.sh */5 * * * * /usr/local/bin/monitor.sh 0 9 * * 1 /usr/local/bin/monday.sh vi /etc/crontab echo '*/15 * * * * root /usr/local/bin/healthcheck.sh' at 10:00 PM atq atrm 1 crontab -l && systemctl is-active crond crontab -u john -l crontab -u john -e systemctl is-enabled crond crontab -r

Systemd Timers (13 commands)

systemctl list-timers systemctl list-timers --all systemctl cat backup.timer systemctl edit backup.timer systemctl start backup.timer systemd-analyze calendar 'Mon..Fri 02:30:00' journalctl -u backup.timer -u backup.service systemctl is-enabled backup.timer && systemctl is-activ systemctl disable --now backup.timer systemctl disable --now backup.timer && rm /etc/systemd cat > /etc/systemd/system/mytask.service << EOF\n[Unit] cat > /etc/systemd/system/mytask.timer << EOF\n[Unit]\n systemctl enable --now mytask.timer

Storage & LVM (44 commands)

lsblk df -h du -sh /var mount | column -t pvs && vgs && lvs pvdisplay /dev/sdb lvdisplay /dev/vg_data/lv_web fdisk /dev/sdb parted /dev/sdb mklabel gpt parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary xfs 1MiB 10GiB parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary 1MiB 100% partprobe /dev/sdb fsck /dev/sdb1 pvcreate /dev/sdb vgcreate vgdata /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 lvcreate -L 5G -n lvdata vgdata lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n lvfull vg_data mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_data/lv_web mkdir -p /mnt/data mount /dev/vg_data/lv_data /mnt/data blkid /dev/vg_data/lv_app echo 'UUID=xxxx-xxxx /mnt/data xfs defaults 0 0' >> /et mount -a vgextend vg0 /dev/sdc lvextend -L +5G /dev/vg_data/lv_web lvextend -L +10G /dev/vg/lv && xfs_growfs /mountpoint resize2fs /dev/vg_data/lv_web fsck /dev/vg_data/lv_app lvcreate -n lv_swap -L 512M vg_data mkswap /dev/vg_data/lv_swap swapon /dev/vg_data/lv_swap swapon -s echo 'UUID=$(blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/vg_data/lv_swa swapoff -a swapoff /dev/vg_data/lv_swap lvcreate -s -n lv_snapshot -L 1G /dev/vg_data/lv_web lvreduce -L -2G /dev/vg_data/lv_web lvremove /dev/vg_data/lv_web vgchange -ay vg_data umount /mnt/app && lvremove -f /dev/vg_data/lv_app && v pvs && vgs && lvs && df -h hdparm -Tt /dev/sda smartctl -a /dev/sda growpart /dev/sda 1

File Systems (13 commands)

mkfs.xfs /dev/sdb1 mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1 mkfs.vfat -F 32 /dev/sdc1 mount /mnt/data findmnt mount -o noexec,nosuid /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data mount -o remount,rw / umount /mnt/data fsck.ext4 /dev/sdb1 xfs_repair /dev/sdb1 df -i tune2fs -l /dev/sdb1 xfs_info /mnt/data

Networking (57 commands)

ip addr show ip link show ip route show ping -c 4 8.8.8.8 ss -tulpn nslookup google.com cat /etc/resolv.conf nmcli con show nmcli dev status nmcli con show eth0 nmcli con mod eth0 ipv4.addresses 192.168.1.100/24 ipv4 nmcli con mod eth0 ipv4.method auto nmcli con up eth0 nmcli con mod eth0 +ipv4.addresses 192.168.1.101/24 nmcli con down eth0 nmcli con add con-name eth1-static ifname eth1 type eth nmcli con delete eth1-static ip route add 10.0.0.0/24 via 192.168.1.254 systemctl reload NetworkManager hostnamectl status hostnamectl set-hostname server.example.com echo '127.0.0.1 server.example.com server' >> /etc/host nmcli connection nmcli connection add type bond con-name bond0 ifname bo nmcli device nmcli connection add type bond-slave con-name bond0-eth nmcli connection modify enp0s3 ipv4.addresses 192.168.1 nmcli connection add type vlan con-name eth0.100 ifname nmcli connection modify enp0s3 ipv4.gateway 192.168.1.1 nmcli connection add type bridge con-name br0 ifname br nmcli connection modify enp0s3 ipv4.dns 8.8.8.8 nmcli dev show eth0 nmcli connection modify enp0s3 ipv4.method manual nmcli connection modify eth0 +ipv4.routes '10.10.10.0/2 nmcli con mod \ nmcli connection down enp0s3 && nmcli connection up enp ip route add 10.10.10.0/24 via 192.168.1.254 dev eth0 ip addr show enp0s3 ip route add 10.0.0.0/8 via 192.168.1.254 nmcli connection modify enp0s3 +ipv4.addresses 10.0.0.2 ip -s link show eth0 nmcli connection modify enp0s3 connection.autoconnect y ip link set eth0 mtu 9000 ls /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ tcpdump -i eth0 -A port 80 cat /etc/hosts cat /etc/hostname cat /etc/resolv.conf cat /etc/nsswitch.conf ping localhost ip addr ifconfig traceroute 8.8.8.8 tcpdump -i enp0s3 nslookup google.com ethtool enp0s3 more /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-enp0s3

Advanced Networking (25 commands)

nmcli connection add type bond con-name bond0 ifname bo nmcli connection add type bond-slave con-name bond0-eth nmcli connection add type vlan con-name eth0.100 ifname nmcli connection add type bridge con-name br0 ifname br nmcli dev show eth0 nmcli connection modify eth0 +ipv4.routes '10.10.10.0/2 nmcli con mod \ ip route add 10.10.10.0/24 via 192.168.1.254 dev eth0 ip route add 10.0.0.0/8 via 192.168.1.254 ip -s link show eth0 ip link set eth0 mtu 9000 ls /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ tcpdump -i eth0 -A port 80 cat /etc/hosts cat /etc/hostname cat /etc/resolv.conf cat /etc/nsswitch.conf ping localhost ip addr ifconfig traceroute 8.8.8.8 tcpdump -i enp0s3 nslookup google.com ethtool enp0s3 more /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-enp0s3

Firewall (26 commands)

systemctl enable --now firewalld firewall-cmd --state firewall-cmd --list-all firewall-cmd --zone=public --list-services firewall-cmd --zone=public --list-ports firewall-cmd --get-default-zone firewall-cmd --get-services firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-service=http firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http firewall-cmd --reload firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-service=https --perman firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-port=8080/tcp firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=8080/tcp firewall-cmd --runtime-to-permanent firewall-cmd --query-service=http firewall-cmd --zone=public --remove-service=http firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=http firewall-cmd --zone=public --remove-port=8080/tcp firewall-cmd --zone=internal --change-interface=eth0 firewall-cmd --list-all-zones firewall-cmd --set-default-zone=internal firewall-cmd --permanent --add-rich-rule='rule family=\ firewall-cmd --set-default-zone=internal --permanent firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-rich-rule='rule family firewall-cmd --zone=external --add-masquerade --permane firewall-cmd --zone=trusted --add-source=192.168.1.0/24

SSH & Remote Access (23 commands)

ssh [email protected] ssh -V ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 ssh-keygen -t ed25519 chmod 700 ~/.ssh && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys ssh-copy-id user@remotehost cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys ssh -T user@remotehost echo 'SSH works' scp file.txt user@remotehost:/home/user/ scp user@remotehost:/path/file.txt . rsync -avz -e ssh /local/dir/ user@remotehost:/remote/d ssh -L 8080:localhost:80 user@remotehost sed -i 's/#Port 22/Port 2222/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config echo 'Host myserver\n HostName server.example.com\n Use sed -i 's/#PermitRootLogin yes/PermitRootLogin no/' /et sed -i 's/#AllowUsers/AllowUsers/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config sshd -t systemctl restart sshd grep 'Failed password' /var/log/secure ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 ssh-copy-id [email protected] ssh [email protected] cd /root/.ssh && ls -l

NFS & Autofs (16 commands)

systemctl enable --now nfs-server echo '/data *(rw,sync)' >> /etc/exports exportfs -av exportfs -ra showmount -e 192.168.1.100 mount -t nfs 192.168.1.100:/share /mnt/nfs echo '192.168.1.100:/share /mnt/nfs nfs defaults 0 0' > firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http mount -a && mount | grep nfs echo '/mnt/auto /etc/auto.misc' >> /etc/auto.master echo 'docs -rw 192.168.1.100:/share/docs' >> /etc/auto. systemctl restart autofs systemctl enable --now autofs ls /mnt/auto/docs systemctl is-enabled nfs-server && exportfs -v umount /mnt/nfs

SELinux (28 commands)

getenforce sestatus selinuxenabled ls -Z /var/www/html ps -Z semanage fcontext -l semanage port -l sed -i 's/SELINUX=enforcing/SELINUX=permissive/' /etc/s setenforce 0 ausearch -m AVC -ts recent ausearch -m avc -ts today ausearch -m AVC -ts recent -c httpd setenforce 1 chcon -t httpd_sys_content_t /web/index.html restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/ getsebool -a getsebool -a | grep ftp setsebool -P httpd_can_network_connect on setsebool -P ftpd_anon_write on setsebool -P httpd_can_network_connect off semanage port -a -t http_port_t -p tcp 8080 audit2allow -M mypolicy < /var/log/audit/audit.log audit2allow -a -M mypolicy semodule -i mypolicy.pp semodule -l touch /.autorelabel semanage fcontext -d '/web(/.*)?' matchpathcon /var/www/html

Containers (39 commands)

podman search nginx podman pull nginx:alpine cat /etc/containers/registries.conf podman images podman run -d --name web -p 8080:80 nginx podman run -d --name web -p 8080:80 -v /web:/usr/share/ podman run -d -v /data/webapp:/var/www/html:Z --name we podman run -d --restart=always --name web nginx:alpine podman run --rm -it nginx bash podman ps -a podman run -d --name web -e DB_HOST=192.168.1.10 -e DB_ podman logs web podman inspect web podman stats podman exec -it web bash podman start web podman stop web podman container prune podman rm web podman rmi nginx podman build -t myapp:v1 . podman tag myapp:v1 registry.example.com/myapp:v1 podman login registry.example.com -u admin podman save -o myapp.tar myapp:v1 podman load -i myapp.tar podman network ls podman network create --subnet 192.168.100.0/24 backend podman volume create mydata podman volume ls podman pod create --name webapp -p 8080:80 podman pod list podman generate systemd --name myapp --files --new loginctl enable-linger username mkdir -p ~/.config/containers/systemd/ cat > ~/.config/containers/systemd/webapp.container << systemctl --user daemon-reload systemctl --user enable --now webapp systemctl --user status webapp /usr/lib/systemd/user-generators/podman-user-generator

Exam Overview

Exam Code: EX200
Duration: 3 hours (180 minutes)
Format: 100% performance-based, hands-on tasks only - no multiple choice
Passing Score: 210 out of 300 points (70%)
Prerequisites: None official. Red Hat recommends completing RH124 and RH134 courses first.
Certification Validity: 3 years from date of passing
Environment: Live RHEL 9 virtual machine. Results published within 3 business days.
Retake Policy: Must wait 7 days before a retake attempt. Unlimited retakes permitted after that.
Delivery: Available at Red Hat testing centers and as a remotely proctored exam from home or office

How the Real EX200 Exam Is Structured

The real exam gives you 15–20 independent tasks displayed as a scrollable task document. They are not a single narrative - but they are written so that earlier tasks create the foundation for later ones. For example:

  • Task 2 creates a user. Task 3 sets permissions for that user. Task 14 configures SSH access for that user.
  • Task 5 creates an LVM logical volume. Task 6 formats and mounts it. Task 16 sets SELinux context on it.
  • Task 7 installs a package. Task 8 enables the service. Task 13 opens the firewall port for it.

Think of it as building a server from scratch - each task adds a layer. You can complete tasks in any order, but skipping an early task often makes a later one impossible. The in-app exam simulates this structure across 20 ordered tasks matching real EX200 categories.

Exam Environment

What to Expect at Login

A RHEL 9 virtual machine desktop environment (GNOME)
The exam task document is displayed alongside the terminal - read it fully before starting
A countdown timer is visible at all times showing remaining time
Internet access is not available during the exam
Man pages and system documentation (man, info, /usr/share/doc) are fully accessible and permitted to use

Terminal Access and Root

Open a terminal from the taskbar or applications menu
Switch to root immediately with sudo -i using the provided password
Open multiple terminal tabs (Ctrl+Shift+T in most terminals) for parallel work
Keep one tab permanently at root for tasks, one tab for checking your own work
All tasks require root access - do not attempt tasks as a regular user

First Five Minutes

  1. Run date to confirm system time is correct
  2. Run ping -c 4 8.8.8.8 to confirm network connectivity if applicable
  3. Run df -h and lsblk to understand available disk layout
  4. Read the entire task list before starting any task - some tasks depend on others
  5. Prioritize tasks: do the ones you are most confident in first to secure points early

SSH Into the Exam System

Connecting to the Exam VM

Some exam formats provide a separate control node and require SSH to reach the exam system. The general pattern:

ssh [email protected] - connect with provided username and hostname
ssh -i ~/.ssh/exam_key [email protected] - connect with a specific key if provided
Type yes to accept the host fingerprint on first connection
Enter the provided password when prompted
Escalate to root immediately with sudo -i once connected

Setting Up Key-Based Authentication

A common exam objective. Complete sequence:

ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f ~/.ssh/id_rsa - generate RSA key pair, press Enter twice for no passphrase
ssh-copy-id user@remotehost - copy public key to remote, enter password once
ssh user@remotehost - verify passwordless login works
cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys - verify the key was installed on the remote
Private key: ~/.ssh/id_rsa | Public key: ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
Fix permissions if key auth fails silently: chmod 700 ~/.ssh && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

SSH File Permissions

~/.ssh directory must be 700 (drwx------): chmod 700 ~/.ssh
~/.ssh/authorized_keys must be 600 (-rw-------): chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
~/.ssh/id_rsa private key must be 600 (-rw-------): chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa
~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub public key is 644 (-rw-r--r--)
Wrong permissions cause silent fallback to password auth with no error message

Hardening SSH

vim /etc/ssh/sshd_config - open config file directly for editing
Change port: find #Port 22, uncomment and change to desired port number
Disable root login: find PermitRootLogin, set to no
Disable password auth: find PasswordAuthentication, set to no
sshd -t - always test config syntax before restarting
systemctl restart sshd - apply changes
If port was changed: firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=2222/tcp && firewall-cmd --reload
Verify new port works before closing the current session

SCP File Transfer

scp file.txt user@remote:/tmp/ - copy local file to remote
scp user@remote:/path/file.txt . - copy remote file to current directory
scp -r /localdir user@remote:/remotedir/ - copy directory recursively
scp -P 2222 file.txt user@remote:/tmp/ - specify non-standard port (capital P)

Troubleshooting SSH

ssh -v user@host - verbose output shows exactly where connection fails
ssh -vvv user@host - maximum verbosity for deep debugging
journalctl -u sshd -f - watch SSH daemon logs in real time
grep 'Failed password' /var/log/secure - view failed login attempts
sestatus then ausearch -m AVC -ts recent - check if SELinux is blocking SSH
firewall-cmd --list-all - verify SSH port is open in firewall
Check ~/.ssh permissions if key auth silently falls back to password

Exam Objectives by Weight

Storage and LVM (20-25%)
Create and manage partitions, physical volumes, volume groups, logical volumes
Create filesystems, configure fstab, mount by UUID
Extend logical volumes and grow filesystems
Configure swap
User and Group Management (15-20%)
Create, modify, and delete users and groups
Configure password aging and account expiry
Set file ownership and permissions
Configure sudo access
SELinux (10-15%)
Set enforcing and permissive modes
Restore file contexts with restorecon
Manage SELinux booleans and port labels
Read audit logs to diagnose denials
Networking (10-15%)
Configure static IP, gateway, and DNS with nmcli
Set hostname and update /etc/hosts
Configure firewall rules
Configure SSH, key-based auth, and sshd settings
Services and Systemd (10-15%)
Enable, start, stop, and check services
Read logs with journalctl
Configure services to start at boot
Essential Tools (10%)
Input/output redirection and pipes
Find, grep, tar, archive and compression
Create and edit files with vim

Critical Commands Per Topic

User Management
useradd -m username - always use -m to create home directory
usermod -aG groupname username - always -aG together, never just -G
chage -M 90 -W 7 username - password aging policy
chown user:group file and chmod 755 file - ownership and permissions
systemd
systemctl enable --now servicename - start and enable in one command
systemctl reload servicename - reload config with no downtime
journalctl -u servicename -f - follow logs
systemctl --failed - see what failed
Networking
nmcli con mod eth0 ipv4.addresses 192.168.1.100/24
nmcli con mod eth0 ipv4.method manual - required when setting static IP
nmcli con up eth0 - apply changes
hostnamectl set-hostname server.example.com
Firewall
firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http
firewall-cmd --reload - always required after --permanent
firewall-cmd --list-all - verify active rules
Storage and LVM
pvcreate /dev/sdb then vgcreate vgname /dev/sdb then lvcreate -L 10G -n lvname vgname
lvextend -r -L +5G /dev/vgname/lvname - -r resizes filesystem automatically
mkfs.xfs /dev/vgname/lvname - RHEL 9 default filesystem
blkid - get UUID for /etc/fstab entry
fstab format: UUID=xxx /mountpoint xfs defaults 0 0
SELinux
restorecon -Rv /path/ - fix context after moving files
setsebool -P boolean_name on - -P makes it persistent across reboots
semanage port -a -t http_port_t -p tcp 8080 - allow non-standard port
ausearch -m AVC -ts recent - see what SELinux blocked

Common Mistakes That Cost Points

Automatic Fail - System Does Not Boot
A bad /etc/fstab entry is the most common cause. Test before rebooting:
mount -a - attempt to mount everything in fstab; errors appear immediately
findmnt --verify - verify all fstab entries are valid
systemctl daemon-reload - reload after any unit file changes
If the system fails to boot after a bad fstab entry, boot into rescue/emergency mode, fix the fstab, then reboot
Always use UUID in fstab, not device names: blkid /dev/vgname/lvname to get UUID, then echo "UUID=xxx /mnt/point xfs defaults 0 0" >> /etc/fstab
Automatic Fail - Wrong Network Config After Reboot
After setting static IP, verify it survives reboot: nmcli con mod eth0 connection.autoconnect yes
Test immediately after applying: nmcli con up eth0 then ip addr show
Ping the gateway to verify routing: ping -c 4 192.168.1.1
Automatic Fail - Locking Yourself Out of SSH
Before disabling password auth or changing SSH port, verify key login works in a separate terminal
Before restarting sshd after config changes: sshd -t to validate the config
If port is changed, open it in firewall first: firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=2222/tcp && firewall-cmd --reload
Keep one existing SSH session open while testing the new config from a second session
Costly Time Wasters
Using usermod -G instead of usermod -aG - destroys all existing group memberships silently
Forgetting firewall-cmd --reload after --permanent - rules are staged but not active
Missing -r flag on lvextend - LV grows but filesystem stays the same size: lvextend -r -L +5G /dev/vg/lv
Not running nmcli con up eth0 after network changes - settings are saved but not applied
Setting SELinux booleans without -P - changes are lost after reboot: setsebool -P httpd_can_network_connect on
Creating a user without -m - home directory is not created: useradd -m username
Forgetting to run systemctl enable servicename separately from start - service runs now but not after reboot. Use enable --now to do both at once

Verification Checklist Before Submission

  1. Reboot the system: systemctl reboot
  2. Verify no services failed: systemctl list-units --state=failed
  3. Verify all mount points: df -h and mount | grep expected_path
  4. Verify network: ip addr show and ping 8.8.8.8
  5. Verify firewall rules: firewall-cmd --list-all
  6. Verify user accounts and groups: id username and getent group groupname
  7. Verify SELinux contexts: ls -Z on configured paths
  8. Verify SSH still connects from a separate terminal
  9. Verify file permissions: ls -l on all configured files

Time Management

First 30 Minutes
Complete all quick tasks: user creation, basic permissions, service enables
Set up networking if required
Open firewall ports for required services
Minutes 30 to 120
Work through medium tasks: LVM setup, NFS, scheduled jobs, SELinux
Test each task immediately after completing it
Final 60 Minutes
Tackle complex troubleshooting tasks
Reboot and verify everything persists
Use the last 15 minutes only for final verification, not new tasks

Remote (At-Home) Exam Tips

Technical Setup - Do These the Day Before
Run the Red Hat compatibility check tool at: redhat.com exam compatibility checker
Test your webcam, microphone, and internet connection - the proctor needs to see you and your workspace
A wired ethernet connection is strongly recommended over WiFi - disconnects can pause or void the exam
Close all unnecessary programs, browser tabs, and background applications before starting
Disable all notifications: Slack, email, calendar alerts, system notifications
Your screen will be shared with the proctor at all times during the exam
Room and Environment Requirements
The room must be quiet and private - no one else can be present
Clear your desk completely: no notebooks, phones, second monitors, sticky notes, or food within camera view
The proctor will ask to scan the room with your webcam before the exam begins
You may not wear headphones or earbuds during the exam
Talking to yourself is allowed; talking to another person is not
You may not leave the camera frame during the exam without notifying the proctor
ID and Check-In
Have a government-issued photo ID ready that matches your Red Hat account name exactly
Join the exam session 15 minutes early - check-in takes time
The proctor will verify your ID and environment before unlocking the exam
During the Exam
If your internet drops, do not panic - reconnect as quickly as possible; the proctor can pause the clock
If you have a technical issue, use the proctor chat to communicate immediately
Do not resize or minimize the exam browser window
Bathroom breaks require proctor approval and may not stop the timer
Community resources for remote exam experiences: Reddit r/redhat, Red Hat Community Forums (forum.redhat.com), and TechExams.net

Exam Day Logistics (Testing Center)

Arrive 15 to 30 minutes early for check-in and ID verification
Bring two valid forms of ID matching the registration name exactly
Personal items go in a locker - nothing is allowed at the desk
Scratch paper may be provided at the center; ask if not available
Skip tasks that are taking too long and return to them later
Partial credit is given - completing part of a task earns some points

Exam Objectives (EX200 Complete List)

Understand and Use Essential Tools

  • Access a shell prompt and issue commands with correct syntax
  • Use input-output redirection: >, >>, |, 2>, and combinations
  • Use grep and regular expressions to analyze text
  • Access remote systems using SSH
  • Log in and switch users in multiuser targets
  • Archive, compress, unpack, and uncompress files using tar, gzip, and bzip2
  • Create and edit text files with vim
  • Create, delete, copy, and move files and directories
  • Create hard and soft links
  • List, set, and change standard ugo/rwx permissions
  • Locate, read, and use system documentation including man, info, and /usr/share/doc

Create Simple Shell Scripts

  • Use conditionals: if, test, [ ]
  • Use looping constructs: for
  • Process script inputs: $1, $2
  • Process output of shell commands within a script

Operate Running Systems

  • Boot, reboot, and shut down a system normally
  • Boot systems into different targets manually
  • Interrupt the boot process to gain access to a system
  • Identify CPU/memory intensive processes and kill processes
  • Adjust process scheduling
  • Manage tuning profiles
  • Locate and interpret system log files and journals
  • Preserve system journals
  • Start, stop, and check the status of network services
  • Securely transfer files between systems

Configure Local Storage

  • List, create, and delete partitions on GPT disks
  • Create and remove physical volumes
  • Assign physical volumes to volume groups
  • Create and delete logical volumes
  • Configure systems to mount file systems at boot by UUID or label
  • Add partitions, logical volumes, and swap non-destructively
  • Create, mount, unmount, and use VFAT, ext4, and xfs file systems
  • Mount and unmount network file systems using NFS
  • Configure autofs
  • Extend existing logical volumes
  • Diagnose and correct file permission problems

Deploy, Configure, and Maintain Systems

  • Schedule tasks using at, cron, and systemd timer units
  • Start and stop services and configure services to start at boot
  • Configure systems to boot into a specific target
  • Configure time service clients
  • Install and update software packages from Red Hat CDN, remote repo, or local file
  • Modify the system bootloader

Manage Basic Networking

  • Configure IPv4 and IPv6 addresses
  • Configure hostname resolution
  • Configure network services to start automatically at boot
  • Restrict network access using firewalld and firewall-cmd

Manage Users and Groups

  • Create, delete, and modify local user accounts
  • Change passwords and adjust password aging
  • Create, delete, and modify local groups and group memberships
  • Configure privileged access

Manage Security

  • Configure firewall settings using firewall-cmd and firewalld
  • Manage default file permissions
  • Configure key-based authentication for SSH
  • Set enforcing and permissive modes for SELinux
  • List and identify SELinux file and process context
  • Restore default file contexts
  • Manage SELinux port labels
  • Use boolean settings to modify SELinux settings
The RHCSA Game Terminal
